title: Crowd Score: A Method for the Evaluation of Jokes using Large Language Model AI Voters as Judges
research_questions: Can a crowd of LLM-based AI voters be used as judges to rate the funniness of jokes without human judges?
How much do zero-shot versus few-shot voting prompts, and the choice of the non-funny label, affect binary joke classification accuracy?
Can humor-style personality induction make different AI voters respond differently to jokes in the expected direction?
Does the aggregated Crowd Score follow the same trend as human judges' funniness ratings?

datasets_used.name: Toplyn [4] joke dataset
datasets_used.size: 13 inputs (headlines), four jokes generated for each input, total of 52 jokes

datasets_used.name: Toplyn [4] four-funniest/four-least-funny subset
datasets_used.size: 8 jokes: the four least funny jokes and the four funniest jokes

methods_proposed.name: Crowd Score
methods_proposed.summary: A method for assessing creative artifacts, instantiated here for jokes, by inducing multiple LLM-based AI voters with different personalities, collecting binary funny/not-funny votes and explanations, validating votes, and aggregating the binary votes into a single score.

methods_proposed.name: LLM-based vote auditing
methods_proposed.summary: For each vote, the LLM is prompted to provide a chain-of-thought-style explanation, and a separate auditing prompt asks whether the reasoning explains the classification. Votes whose explanations fail the audit are discarded before aggregation.

humor_theories.detail: The four induced voter personalities are based on affiliative, self-enhancing, aggressive and self-defeating humor types from Martin et al. [16].

evaluation_methods.detail: GPT-3 text-davinci-002 is used as AI voters with induced humor-style personalities and also as the automated auditor of vote explanations.
F-score and balanced accuracy are used to evaluate binary funny versus opposite-label voting prompts on an 8-joke subset and the full 52-joke dataset.
Human judges' ratings from Toplyn [4], on a 1 to 4 scale, are used to sort jokes, define funny/not-funny labels using a threshold of 2, and compare against the Crowd Score trend.
A human expert inspected the auditing results and found that around 40% of votes invalidated by the current auditing prompt should be considered valid.

headline_results.finding: On the 8-joke voting-question subset, Funny/Boring and Funny/Dull were the best label pairs.
headline_results.best_system: GPT-3 (text-davinci-002) with Funny/Boring or Funny/Dull prompt

headline_results.finding: On the full 52-joke dataset, Funny/Boring outperformed the naive Funny/Not Funny label pair, especially in zero-shot prompting.
headline_results.value: Funny/Boring: 0.78 and 0.7 (zero-shot), 0.83 and 0.78 (few-shot); Funny/Not Funny: 0.59 and 0.6 (zero-shot), 0.81 and 0.76 (few-shot)

headline_results.finding: Picking the least appropriate opposite word substantially reduced balanced accuracy.
headline_results.value: 26% and 25% for zero-shot and few-shot respectively

headline_results.finding: The automated auditing step invalidated a minority of all votes, but the authors report that it was over-conservative.
headline_results.value: 14% of all votes; around 40% of votes that it still invalidates should be considered valid

headline_results.finding: For the funniest and least funny jokes, the Crowd Score largely separated high- and low-rated items.
headline_results.value: for the top 10 jokes, only one score in 20 was below 3; for the bottom 10 jokes, only 5 in 20 scores above 3

key_findings: Few-shot prompting generally improves voting-question accuracy, but the choice of the opposite word for funny also matters substantially.
On the full dataset, the Funny/Boring prompt achieved F-Score 0.78 and balanced accuracy 0.7 in zero-shot, compared with 0.59 and 0.6 for the naive Funny/Not Funny prompt.

Personality induction produced the expected qualitative pattern: aggressive and self-defeating AI voters were more inclined to find the aggressive/self-defeating joke set funny than affiliative and self-enhancing voters.
Few-shot examples interfered with personality induction, because the paper reports that few-shot prompting overwrites the personality induction and makes all personalities achieve similar results.
The Crowd Score trend follows human ratings, and it was fairly accurate at detecting the funniest and least funny jokes according to the paper's top-10 and bottom-10 analysis.
The current auditing prompt invalidated 14% of votes, but a human expert inspection suggested that around 40% of invalidated votes should have been considered valid.
limitations: The empirical study is a small case study with 52 jokes and four induced humor-style personalities.
All jokes in the dataset are described as being within the aggressive/self-defeating spectrum, so the personality-induction findings are tied to that joke type.
The best voting question was found manually; the paper lists automated discovery of voting questions as future work.
Few-shot prompting can overwrite the intended personality induction, making the personality setup fragile.
The same LLM is used to generate and audit explanations, and the paper notes that another model could be beneficial to avoid self-evaluation bias.
The current auditing prompt is over-conservative, with a human expert judging that around 40% of invalidated votes should be considered valid.
The paper only studies simple binary vote summation for aggregation and leaves other aggregation functions for future work.

human_samples.population: Human judges whose funniness ratings are included in the Toplyn [4] dataset
human_samples.observations: Funniness ratings on a 1-to-4 scale for the 52 jokes were used to sort jokes, select the four least funny and four funniest jokes, define binary labels using a threshold of 2, and plot human-rating trends against Crowd Score.
human_samples.detail: These ratings were reused from Toplyn [4]; this paper does not report the number of human judges, their recruitment, geography, demographics, or rating protocol beyond the 1-to-4 scale.

human_samples.observations: Inspected the auditing results and found that the current auditing prompt captured positives but that around 40% of votes it still invalidated should be considered valid.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not identify the expert, describe their expertise, report blinding, or state how many invalidated votes were inspected.

human_samples.population: Professional human comedian in the external Toplyn [4] dataset
human_samples.observations: The Toplyn [4] dataset included jokes generated by a professional human comedian, alongside jokes generated by OpenAI GPT-3, Witscript, and Witscript 2, for 13 inputs/headlines.
human_samples.detail: This was an external dataset source rather than a participant recruited for the present paper.

judges.name: GPT-3 text-davinci-002 AI voters
judges.detail: Used as the crowd of AI judges to classify each joke as Funny or an opposite label, and in the Crowd Score experiments as four induced humour-type voters: affiliative, self-enhancing, aggressive, and self-defeating.

judges.name: GPT-3 text-davinci-002 auditing prompt
judges.detail: Used to answer whether a vote explanation explained why the joke had the given classification; votes receiving a negative audit were discarded for the valid-vote Crowd Score.

judges.name: Toplyn [4] human judges
judges.detail: Their external funniness ratings supplied the reference ordering, the four-funniest/four-least-funny subset, the binary funny/not-funny labels for F-score and balanced accuracy, and the human trend plotted against Crowd Score.
An unnamed human expert inspected auditing outcomes and judged that around 40% of votes still invalidated by the audit prompt should be valid.

judges.name: F-score, balanced accuracy, summed/normalized Crowd Score
judges.detail: F-score and balanced accuracy were used for binary voting-question accuracy; binary AI votes were summed into Crowd Score and normalized to the [1,4] range for comparison with human ratings.

generation_selection.detail: The authors did not run a joke-generation experiment; they evaluated 52 jokes from Toplyn [4]. The four-funniest/four-least-funny subset was selected by external human ratings for voting-question tuning, not by best-of-N generation.

model_provenance.name: GPT-3
model_provenance.detail: Used for all reported LLM voting, explanation, and auditing experiments. The paper says GPT-3 is provided by OpenAI, but does not report API details, access path, inference date, or training cutoff.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: All experiments were run 3 times and the reported results are arithmetic means; exact call counts are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: No max-token limit, stop sequences, random seeds, retry policy, latency, cost, or total compute/API usage is reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Not explicitly reported; the described design tests zero-shot and few-shot prompts across 8 opposite labels, with all experiments run 3 times.
inference_budgets.detail: The subset contains the four least funny and four funniest jokes by human rating. Opposite labels tested were Not funny, Dumb, Unfunny, Not Amusing, Sad, Serious, Dull, and Boring.

inference_budgets.scope: Voting-question evaluation on the full 52-joke dataset
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Not explicitly reported; the described design tests Boring and Not Funny as the opposite labels under zero-shot and few-shot prompting, with all experiments run 3 times.
inference_budgets.detail: Binary labels were derived from human ratings: ratings >= 2 were considered funny and ratings < 2 not funny, yielding 37 funny and 15 not-funny jokes.

inference_budgets.scope: Personality induction, auditing, and Crowd Score on the 52-joke dataset
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Not explicitly reported; the method uses four personality votes per joke, vote explanations, and an audit prompt for each vote, with all experiments run 3 times.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Four AI-voter personalities per joke: affiliative, self-enhancing, aggressive, and self-defeating; these are judges, not best-of-N candidates selected for output quality.
inference_budgets.detail: Personality induction used the zero-shot version because the paper reports that few-shot examples overwrite the personality induction and make personalities achieve similar results.

human_baselines.scope: Human funniness ratings versus Crowd Score
human_baselines.description: Human ratings from Toplyn [4] on a 1-to-4 scale were used as the reference trend and label source for evaluating the AI Crowd Score on the same 52 jokes.
human_baselines.detail: The same joke items are compared, but the human ratings come from an external study with unreported participant/procedure details, while the model score is a normalized sum of binary AI votes rather than the same 1-to-4 rating task.

human_baselines.scope: Human comedian as joke source in Toplyn [4]
human_baselines.description: The evaluated dataset includes jokes produced by a professional human comedian, and the paper notes that Toplyn [4] found human jokes scored highest while GPT-3 jokes scored lowest.
human_baselines.detail: The present paper does not run a matched human-versus-model generation evaluation; it uses the external 52-joke dataset as evaluation material.

mitigations: The authors selected the Toplyn [4] dataset because it contains recent jokes that they state were not included in the training dataset of the target GPT-3 model.
The dataset contains non-trivial jokes relying on common sense rather than wordplay, according to the paper.
contamination_risk.basis: The paper explicitly asserts that the recent Toplyn [4] jokes were not in GPT-3 training data, but it reports no GPT-3 training cutoff, inference date, API snapshot beyond text-davinci-002, or decontamination test. The evaluation uses an external public 2022 dataset and a proprietary GPT-3 model, and some evaluated jokes were generated by OpenAI GPT-3 in the source dataset, so residual memorization or source familiarity cannot be ruled out.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: The central claims about Crowd Score as a joke-evaluation method, voting-question accuracy, personality-induction differences, auditing invalidation rates, and the Crowd Score trend all depend on GPT-3 outputs as voters and/or auditor.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: External human ratings from Toplyn [4] provide reference labels/trends, and one unnamed human expert inspected auditing outcomes and judged that around 40% of still-invalidated votes should be valid.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLM judgments are not merely diagnostic; they constitute the proposed metric and determine the reported AI votes, vote explanations, audit-validity filtering, and aggregated Crowd Scores.

reporting_gaps: The number, recruitment source, demographics, geography, and detailed rating procedure of the Toplyn [4] human judges are not reported in this paper.
The identity, expertise criteria, blinding status, annotation protocol, and number of cases inspected by the human expert are not reported.
The paper reports GPT-3 text-davinci-002 but not the API/access path, inference dates, training cutoff, or whether the model snapshot could have changed.
No decontamination test is reported for the Toplyn [4] jokes or their human ratings, despite the claim that the jokes were not in GPT-3 training data.
Exact API call counts, max-token limits, stop sequences, random seeds, retry/failure handling, latency, cost, and compute budget are not reported.
The concrete few-shot examples used in the voting-question prompts are templated but not fully specified in the paper text.
The manual process for choosing the set of opposite words is not described beyond listing the words tested.
Exact numeric counts from the personality-vote and invalidated-vote bar charts are not provided in the markdown text, aside from the overall 14% invalidated-vote rate.

The paper does not report confidence intervals or statistical-test details for the statement that aggressive and self-defeating voters are significantly more inclined to find the aggressive/self-defeating jokes funny.
